statement: Iceland's general consumer price level was approximately 50% above the EU-27 average in 2024, according to Eurostat comparative price level indices. This is the highest in Europe after Switzerland. By comparison, Denmark was 40% above, Norway 30% above, Sweden 15% above, and Finland 20% above the EU average.
source_name: Eurostat — Comparative price levels of consumer goods and services (prc_ppp_ind)
caveats: High price levels reflect multiple factors beyond trade policy: small domestic market, high wages, transport costs, taxes, and limited competition. EU membership would reduce some price differences (agricultural tariffs, rules of origin) but would not eliminate the structural factors driving Iceland's high price level. Norway remains expensive despite EEA membership.

related_entries.statement: Iceland's GDP per capita (PPP) was approximately $72,000 in 2024, ranking among the highest in Europe. This compares to Denmark ($73,000), Sweden ($65,000), Finland ($57,000), Norway ($88,000), and the EU-27 average of approximately $56,000. Iceland's GDP per capita (PPP) is roughly 29% above the EU average.

Food and non-alcoholic beverage prices in Iceland are approximately 50–70% higher than the EU-27 average, according to Eurostat comparative price level indices (2024). This makes Iceland one of the most expensive countries in Europe for food.
